request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"knewton::edtech","name":"Knewton","one_liner":"Adaptive learning SaaS platform that personalized educational content for universities, publishers, and developers via an API/licensing model.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both are B2B SaaS platforms licensed to institutional/organizational customers on a subscription basis, but Knewton sold to education publishers and universities while the new pitch targets academic spinouts and small CROs in biotech. The licensing-to-institutions pattern is shared; the vertical and buyer profile differ materially."},"market":{"score":1,"rationale":"Knewton operated in the edtech/adaptive-learning market targeting higher education and K-12 publishers. The new pitch is in the biotech/life-sciences sector targeting wet-lab assay management. These are entirely different verticals with different regulatory dynamics, buyer personas, and competitive landscapes."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both rely on B2B enterprise-style partnerships and licensing agreements to reach end users (Knewton via Pearson, HMH, Macmillan; the new pitch via academic spinouts and small CROs). Channel-partnership-heavy GTM is a rough parallel, though biotech SaaS GTM typically runs through lab procurement channels rather than publishing conglomerates."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Knewton raised over $150M over multiple rounds before a distressed acquisition at under $17M, suggesting premature scaling before product-market fit was proven. The new pitch appears to be at an early stage; both involve enterprise SaaS platforms that require significant adoption cycles with institutional buyers."}},"why_similar":"Both are B2B SaaS licensing platforms sold to institutional customers on a recurring-subscription model. Knewton built an API/platform layer that third-party organizations (publishers, universities) integrated into their own products — structurally analogous to a lab-management platform licensed to academic spinouts and CROs. Both face the challenge of selling relatively complex software to buyers who are domain experts (educators; lab scientists) but not software-native, and both depend on institutional partnerships for distribution scale.","where_diverged":"The new pitch diverges on every vertical dimension: (1) Sector — biotech/life sciences vs. edtech; regulatory and compliance obligations (GxP, 21 CFR Part 11) in the new pitch have no equivalent in Knewton's market. (2) Customer profile — academic spinouts and small CROs are smaller, more technically specialized, and often grant-funded, versus Knewton's large publishing conglomerates and universities with multi-year budget cycles. (3) Product domain — wet-lab assay management involves instrument integration, data provenance, and audit trails that are structurally different from adaptive-content delivery. (4) Scale ambition — Knewton pursued hypergrowth internationally; a niche CRO/spinout platform may be designed for a tighter, defensible segment with lower capital intensity.","failure_causes":["over-reliance on a small number of mega-publisher partners (Pearson, Macmillan) for distribution","raised ~$157M but sold for under $17M — massive capital inefficiency and likely premature international scaling","inability to prove measurable learning-outcome ROI at scale, undermining renewal and expansion","platform-layer commoditization as publishers and universities built in-house adaptive tools","transition from B2B API platform to direct courseware product (2016) created internal strategic confusion","long enterprise sales cycles consuming capital before meaningful recurring revenue","distressed acquisition suggesting failure to achieve standalone sustainable unit economics"],"lessons_for_input":["Avoid over-concentration in a handful of anchor partners — if one Pearson-equivalent CRO or publisher walks, your ARR collapses; diversify your customer base early.","Prove measurable lab-efficiency ROI (e.g., reduced assay error rates, faster turnaround) before scaling headcount or pursuing international expansion.","Resist the temptation to pivot from platform-licensing to building your own end-product (Knewton's 2016 courseware pivot burned capital and confused the market).","Right-size your fundraise to your market: small CROs and academic spinouts are a narrower TAM than K-12 publishing — raise to milestones, not to perceived prestige rounds.","Build compliance features (audit trails, 21 CFR Part 11, GxP) as a moat from day one; unlike edtech, biotech buyers will not purchase without them."]}
